children.name: Tinamus
children.description: The largest tinamou genus, comprising heavily-built, terrestrial forest birds with cryptic plumage and a distinctive curved bill. They are weak fliers but extremely difficult to spot on the forest floor.

children.name: Tinamus major
children.description: The largest tinamou, reaching 44 centimetres in length. It ranges from southern Mexico through Central America to the Amazon Basin, inhabiting lowland tropical forests. Its mournful, whistled song is heard far more often than the bird is seen as it creeps silently through the undergrowth.

children.name: Tinamus guttatus
children.description: A handsome tinamou of the Amazon rainforest, distinguished by its white throat and breast contrasting with a rich brown back. It prefers terra firme forests away from rivers and is known for its clear, descending whistle. Like all tinamous, it lays brilliantly coloured eggs with a glossy shell.

children.name: Tinamus osgoodi
children.description: A large black tinamou of the cloud forests of Peru and Bolivia.

children.name: Tinamus bensoni
children.description: A tinamou found in the lowland forests of western Amazonia.

children.name: Tinamus serratus
children.description: A tinamou found in the Atlantic Forest of Brazil.

children.name: Tinamus tao
children.description: A large grey tinamou found in the Amazon basin.

children.name: Tinamus solitarius
children.description: A large tinamou endemic to the Atlantic Forest of Brazil.

children.name: Nothoprocta
children.description: A genus of medium-sized tinamous adapted to the open grasslands, scrublands and high-altitude puna of the Andes. Their mottled plumage offers superb camouflage against the rocky, arid landscapes they inhabit.

children.name: Nothoprocta perdicaria
children.description: Endemic to Chile, this stout-bodied tinamou inhabits the matorral shrublands and agricultural areas from the Atacama to the Bio-Bio region. Its cryptic grey-brown plumage, streaked with buff and black, renders it nearly invisible among dry grasses. It feeds on seeds and insects gleaned from the ground.

children.name: Nothoprocta ornata
children.description: Found across the high Andes from Peru to northwestern Argentina, this tinamou occupies grassy puna and rocky slopes at elevations of 3,000 to 4,500 metres. Its plumage is intricately patterned with black, buff and chestnut bars. It runs swiftly uphill when disturbed rather than taking flight.

children.name: Nothoprocta pentlandii
children.description: Widely distributed along the Andes from Ecuador to Argentina, this smaller tinamou favours arid valleys and montane scrub between 1,500 and 4,000 metres. Its plaintive, flutelike call is a characteristic sound of the Andean foothills. It is more often heard than seen, relying on immobility and camouflage to evade detection.

children.name: Nothoprocta cinerascens
children.description: A tinamou of scrublands in southern South America.

children.name: Nothoprocta curvirostris
children.description: A tinamou with curved bill, found in the high Andes.

children.name: Nothoprocta taczanowskii
children.description: A tinamou found in the high Andes of Peru and Bolivia.

children.name: Eudromia
children.description: Distinguished by their elegant, forward-curving crests, these larger tinamous inhabit dry open country in southern South America. They are more social than other tinamous, often encountered in small coveys.

children.name: Eudromia elegans
children.description: A strikingly beautiful bird with a long, thin crest curving forward over its bill and fine vermiculations covering its buff and brown plumage. It ranges across the pampas and Monte Desert of Argentina and Chile. Gregarious outside the breeding season, it forms flocks of up to a dozen birds foraging for seeds and insects.

children.name: Eudromia formosa
children.description: A crested tinamou of the Gran Chaco region in South America.

children.name: Rhynchotus
children.description: Medium-sized tinamous with a distinctive decurved bill and reddish primary feathers that flash in flight. They occupy open grasslands and savannas across much of South America.

children.name: Rhynchotus rufescens
children.description: A common and widespread tinamou of the Brazilian cerrado, the Pantanal and Argentinian pampas. Its russet wings, which flash conspicuously in flight, are its most distinctive feature. It adapts readily to agricultural landscapes and its whistled song is a familiar sound across the South American grasslands.

children.name: Nothura
children.description: Small tinamous of open grasslands and savannas in South America.

children.name: Nothura boraquira
children.description: A nothura with white belly, found in the Gran Chaco.

children.name: Nothura chacoensis
children.description: A nothura of the Gran Chaco region.

children.name: Nothura maculosa
children.description: A spotted nothura found across much of South America.

children.name: Nothura marmorata
children.description: A marbled nothura found in southern South America.

children.name: Nothura minor
children.description: A small nothura endemic to the cerrado of Brazil.

children.name: Nothura nigra
children.description: A nothura with black markings, found in southern Brazil.

children.name: Taoniscus
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the smallest tinamou.

children.name: Taoniscus nanus
children.description: The smallest tinamou, found in the cerrado of Brazil. Considered endangered.

children.name: Nothocercus
children.description: A genus of tinamous found in the montane forests of Central and South America.

children.name: Nothocercus bonapartei
children.description: A tinamou found in the highlands of Costa Rica and western Panama.

children.name: Nothocercus julius
children.description: A tinamou with tawny breast, found in the Andes from Colombia to Peru.

children.name: Nothocercus nigrocapillus
children.description: A tinamou with black cap, found in the Andes of Peru and Bolivia.

children.name: Rhynchotus
children.description: Medium-sized tinamous with reddish primary feathers, found in open country.

children.name: Rhynchotus rufescens
children.description: A common tinamou of grasslands across South America.

children.name: Rhynchotus maculicollis
children.description: A tinamou found in the Andes of Bolivia and Argentina.

children.name: Crypturellus
children.description: The most diverse genus of tinamous, with many species found across Central and South America.

children.name: Crypturellus atrocapillus
children.description: A tinamou with black cap, found in the lowland forests of Peru and Bolivia.

children.name: Crypturellus bartletti
children.description: A tinamou found in the western Amazon basin.

children.name: Crypturellus berlepschi
children.description: A tinamou found in the Choco region of Colombia and Ecuador.

children.name: Crypturellus boucardi
children.description: A tinamou with slaty head, found from Mexico to Costa Rica.
